pick.rationale: Duke enters as the undisputed #1 team in both KenPom and Torvik with an elite two-way profile—4th in adjusted offense and 1st in adjusted defense—while Michigan State's defensive inconsistencies were exposed in their Big Ten Tournament quarterfinal loss to UCLA where they allowed 63% first-half shooting. The Blue Devils already proved their superiority with a 66-60 victory in East Lansing earlier this season, when Cameron Boozer dominated with 18 points and 15 rebounds, and Duke's suffocating defense should neutralize a Michigan State offense that has struggled against elite competition. While Tom Izzo's legendary March pedigree (28 consecutive NCAA appearances, 8 Final Fours) gives the Spartans a credible path to keep it competitive, Duke's substantial analytical edge, head-to-head victory, superior momentum from their second straight ACC championship, and the Boozer twins' dominant play make them the clear favorite to advance to the Final Four.

result.text: Three takeaways on Michigan State basketball's draw in NCAA Tournament Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Women's Basketball Hockey Tom Izzo Recruiting Big Ten Sports Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Three takeaways on Michigan State basketball's draw in NCAA Tournament Robert Bondy Spartans Wire Michigan State basketball is back in the NCAA Tournament, and it'll take arguably one of Tom Izzo's best coaching jobs to reach another Final Four this year. The Spartans bowed out early in the Big Ten Tournament and suffered the punishment of that when the NCAA Tournament bracket was released on Sunday. Michigan State was passed by Purdue on Sunday, and landed as the No. 3 seed in the East Region. The Spartans will open the NCAA Tournament against No. 14 seed North Dakota State on Thursday in Buffalo, N.Y. With the bracket set in place, here are a few of my initial takeaways on the NCAA Tournament draw for the Spartans: MSU got what it deserved after UCLA loss Obviously, I didn't want to see the Spartans drop from the No. 2 seed and a much more favorable draw in the West Region. But I'd be lying if I said they didn't deserve to be punished for that poor outing against the Bruins on Friday. Michigan State losing its Big Ten Tournament opener combined with Purdue winning four games in four days to take the tournament crown ultimately was the reason why the Spartans landed as the No. 3 seed in the East Region. It's only one seed line but it's a notable difference when you look at the potential path to a Final Four and the Spartans now ending up in the hardest region in the field. Michigan State came into this weekend probably needing just one win to seal up a No. 2 seed but they didn't get the job done, and now will have to pay the punishment with a harder draw. I wish it was different but it is what it is and now it's time to move on to what is ahead. Spartans landed in brutally tough, but fun, region If you are looking for star power and blue blood programs, then look no further than the East Region. The top eight seeds in the Big Ten read as the following: No. 1 Duke, No. 2 UConn, No. 3 Michigan State, No. 4 Kansas, No. 5 St. John's, No. 6 Louisville. No. 7 UCLA and No. 8 Ohio State. That's a lot of programs and head coaches that have reached Final Fours and won national championships. Should Michigan State get past the Bison in the opening round, there is a legit possibility they'll need to beat three teams in a row that were all ranked in the top 10 national for extended periods of the season. That would include Louisville in the second round, UConn in the Sweet 16 and Duke (or maybe Kansas or St. John's) in the Elite Eight. That's obviously a long ways away from right now, but it simply shows how difficult it'll be to come out of this region. result.text: Duke 66-60 Michigan State (Dec 6, 2025) Game Recap - ESPN Skip to main content Skip to navigation ESPN NFL NFL NBA NBA NCAAM NCAAM NCAAW NCAAW MLB MLB NHL NHL Soccer Soccer More Sports More Sports Watch Watch Fantasy Fantasy Where to Watch Search Duke Blue Devils @ Michigan State Spartans 4 Duke Blue Devils Duke DUKE 10-0 , 2-0 Away 66 10-0 , 2-0 Away Final 1 2 T Duke DUKE 31 35 66 Michigan St MSU 34 26 60 7 Michigan State Spartans Michigan St MSU 8-1 , 5-1 Home 60 8-1 , 5-1 Home Gamecast Recap Box Score Play-by-Play Team Stats Cameron Boozer scores 18 in No. 4 Duke's 66-60 win over No. 7 Michigan State By AP Dec 8, 2026, 01:12 am Facebook Facebook Messenger Twitter Email EAST LANSING, Mich. -- — Cameron Boozer scored 18 points, including 16 in the second half, and Caleb Foster added 12 points Saturday to help No. 4 Duke hold on for a 66-60 victory over No. 7 Michigan State in a game that had the intensity of an NCAA Regional Final. Boozer, who entered averaging nearly 27 points a game, also had 15 rebounds for the Blue Devils (10-0). Duke's start is its best open to a season since winning the first 11 games in 2017-18. A combination of missed open shots and tight defense kept the game close. The teams traded the lead nine times and were tied four times in the second half. Isaiah Evans gave the Blue Devils a 55-53 lead with 3:59 remaining, but Jeremy Fears tied it at 55 with a pair of free throws with 1:59 left. Boozer then hit two free throws with 1:35 to go to put Duke up for good. Evans then was fouled by Fears on a 3-point shot and made all three free throws to give Duke a 60-55 advantage. Carson Cooper had a layup with 46 seconds left to cut the margin to 60-57 but that was as close as Michigan State could get. Cooper led Michigan State (8-1) with 16 points and a career-high 16 rebounds. Jaxon Kohler had 14 points and seven rebounds for the Spartans. Neither team could get into an offensive rhythm in the first half. Duke led through the first 12 minutes, up by as many as six points. Michigan State grabbed the lead on a 3-pointer by Jesse McCulloch with 5:02 left and held on for a 34-31 halftime advantage. Both teams struggled with their shooting from the field in the first half, combining for 22 of 63. Up next Duke: Hosts Lipscomb on Dec. 16. The Spartans trailed throughout the night en route to a disappointing 88-84 loss to UCLA in the Big Ten Tournament quarterfinals on Friday. Michigan State beat UCLA by more than 20 points less than a month ago, but a poor performance from the Spartans combined with outstanding shooting from the Bruins paved way to an upset loss on Friday. Michigan State left its defense in East Lansing, Mich. in the first half, with one of their worst defensive efforts in an opening half of the season. UCLA shot 63 percent from the field, including above 50 percent from three-point range in the first half, with three different Bruins scoring nine or more points. That strong offensive effort pushed UCLA to a 44-33 lead at halftime. Michigan State would rally at multiple times throughout the second half, but never took the lead with UCLA continuously hitting big shot after big shot to counter. Ultimately, that was enough to push the Bruins forward to the win. Michigan State will now await their fate in the NCAA Tournament, with the Spartans in line for either a No. 2 or No. 3 seed. A win over the Bruins would have certainly locked up a No. 2 seed but now the Spartans will have to wait until Selection Sunday to see where they land. result.text: MSU basketball plays poor, loses vs. UCLA in Big Ten Tournament Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Women's Basketball Hockey Tom Izzo Recruiting Big Ten Sports Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports MSU basketball plays poor, loses vs. UCLA in Big Ten Tournament Robert Bondy Spartans Wire Michigan State basketball laid an egg on Friday night in a disappointing showing at the Big Ten Tournament. The Spartans trailed throughout the night en route to a disappointing 88-84 loss to UCLA in the Big Ten Tournament quarterfinals on Friday. Michigan State beat UCLA by more than 20 points less than a month ago, but a poor performance from the Spartans combined with outstanding shooting from the Bruins paved way to an upset loss on Friday. Michigan State left its defense in East Lansing, Mich. in the first half, with one of their worst defensive efforts in an opening half of the season. UCLA shot 63 percent from the field, including above 50 percent from three-point range in the first half, with three different Bruins scoring nine or more points. That strong offensive effort pushed UCLA to a 44-33 lead at halftime. Michigan State would rally at multiple times throughout the second half, but never took the lead with UCLA continuously hitting big shot after big shot to counter. Ultimately, that was enough to push the Bruins forward to the win. Michigan State will now await their fate in the NCAA Tournament, with the Spartans in line for either a No. 2 or No. 3 seed. A win over the Bruins would have certainly locked up a No. 2 seed but now the Spartans will have to wait until Selection Sunday to see where they land.
